question: A 76-year-old woman presented with 2 weeks of generalized weakness, recurrent syncope, shortness of breath, and fever. Laboratory testing showed leukocytosis and an elevated D-dimer, and computed tomography angiography was obtained to evaluate for pulmonary embolism. Based on the image, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial CT pulmonary angiogram image for anatomic and attenuation details. I see a discrete, rounded intraluminal filling defect projecting into the main pulmonary artery near the bifurcation (arrow). The lesion is heterogeneous in density: there is higher-attenuation contrast at its periphery that is contiguous with the enhancing blood pool, and a relatively lower-attenuation center, so the defect is not a uniformly low-density, non-opacified cast of thrombus.

Next, I relate those imaging findings to the clinical question. The patient has fever and leukocytosis in addition to dyspnea, syncope, and an elevated D-dimer. Those systemic inflammatory/infectious signs make an infectious intravascular process a strong consideration when an intraluminal mass shows internal heterogeneity and some contrast perfusion at its margin.

Then I consider how each option aligns with the imaging and clinical picture. A bland (acute) thrombus would more typically appear as a uniformly non-enhancing filling defect conforming to the lumen without internal contrast infiltration; that does not match the heterogeneous, partially opacified mass seen here. A pulmonary artery sarcoma or intraluminal metastasis could produce an enhancing mass, but these entities are less concordant with the acute systemic infectious signs (fever, leukocytosis) and the rounded intraluminal appearance that can represent an infected vegetation or septic embolus. The combination of an intraluminal, partially contrast-opacified rounded mass in the pulmonary artery plus fever and leukocytosis is most consistent with a septic pulmonary artery vegetation.

Therefore, integrating the image appearance (rounded, heterogeneous intraluminal lesion with peripheral contrast) and the clinical signs of infection, the most likely diagnosis is septic pulmonary artery vegetation.
caption: Computed tomography angiogram of the main pulmonary artery showing vegetation (arrow).
sub_caption: Computed tomography angiogram of the main pulmonary artery showing vegetation (arrow).
image_context: A 76-year-old woman with osteoporosis, hypothyroidism, and dyslipidemia presented to the emergency room with generalized weakness, recurrent syncope, and shortness of breath of 2 weeks’ duration. The patient denied any history of clots, smoking, or surgery. On presentation, she was febrile, tachycardic, mildly hypertensive, and hypoxic with an oxygen saturation of 86% on room air and needing 5 L of oxygen to maintain saturation. The pulmonary and cardiovascular examination findings were unremarkable. Her laboratory findings were significant for leukocytosis and elevated brain natriuretic peptide and D-dimer levels. Blood cultures were performed due to concerns for infection, and computed tomography angiography (CTA; <xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig 1</xref>) was performed to rule out PE. CTA of the chest confirmed an intraluminal filling defect in the pulmonary artery that was concerning for thrombus vs a mass. Therapeutic anticoagulation was initiated. On careful investigation of the images, contrast was seen infiltrating within the filling defect, an unusual finding for a PE. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) performed by our cardiology colleagues revealed right-sided heart strain with associated tricuspid regurgitation. It also revealed a 2.3-cm heterogenous mobile mass in the pulmonary artery without valve involvement. The findings were unequivocal, and the patient remained symptomatic. Vascular surgery was consulted, and the patient was scheduled for percutaneous transluminal mechanical thrombectomy with the goals of symptom resolution and resection of a tissue specimen for pathologic examination.) was performed to rule out PE. CTA of the chest confirmed an intraluminal filling defect in the pulmonary artery that was concerning for thrombus vs a mass. Therapeutic anticoagulation was initiated. On careful investigation of the images, contrast was seen infiltrating within the filling defect, an unusual finding for a PE. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) performed by our cardiology colleagues revealed right-sided heart strain with associated tricuspid regurgitation. It also revealed a 2.3-cm heterogenous mobile mass in the pulmonary artery without valve involvement. The findings were unequivocal, and the patient remained symptomatic. Vascular surgery was consulted, and the patient was scheduled for percutaneous transluminal mechanical thrombectomy with the goals of symptom resolution and resection of a tissue specimen for pathologic examination.Fig 1Computed tomography angiogram of the main pulmonary artery showing vegetation (arrow).
relevant_image_context: A 76-year-old woman with osteoporosis, hypothyroidism, and dyslipidemia presented to the emergency room with generalized weakness, recurrent syncope, and shortness of breath of 2 weeks’ duration. The patient denied any history of clots, smoking, or surgery. On presentation, she was febrile, tachycardic, mildly hypertensive, and hypoxic with an oxygen saturation of 86% on room air and needing 5 L of oxygen to maintain saturation. The pulmonary and cardiovascular examination findings were unremarkable. Her laboratory findings were significant for leukocytosis and elevated brain natriuretic peptide and D-dimer levels. Blood cultures were performed due to concerns for infection, and computed tomography angiography (CTA; Fig 1) was performed to rule out PE. CTA of the chest confirmed an intraluminal filling defect in the pulmonary artery that was concerning for thrombus vs a mass. Therapeutic anticoagulation was initiated. On careful investigation of the images, contrast was seen infiltrating within the filling defect, an unusual finding for a PE. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) performed by our cardiology colleagues revealed right-sided heart strain with associated tricuspid regurgitation. It also revealed a 2.3-cm heterogenous mobile mass in the pulmonary artery without valve involvement. The findings were unequivocal, and the patient remained symptomatic. Vascular surgery was consulted, and the patient was scheduled for percutaneous transluminal mechanical thrombectomy with the goals of symptom resolution and resection of a tissue specimen for pathologic examination.) was performed to rule out PE. CTA of the chest confirmed an intraluminal filling defect in the pulmonary artery that was concerning for thrombus vs a mass. Therapeutic anticoagulation was initiated. On careful investigation of the images, contrast was seen infiltrating within the filling defect, an unusual finding for a PE. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) performed by our cardiology colleagues revealed right-sided heart strain with associated tricuspid regurgitation. It also revealed a 2.3-cm heterogenous mobile mass in the pulmonary artery without valve involvement. The findings were unequivocal, and the patient remained symptomatic. Vascular surgery was consulted, and the patient was scheduled for percutaneous transluminal mechanical thrombectomy with the goals of symptom resolution and resection of a tissue specimen for pathologic examination.Fig 1Computed tomography angiogram of the main pulmonary artery showing vegetation (arrow).
